data.title: China's AI Boom: From Compute Constraints To Commercial Momentum
data.description:
For investors, AI in China is not simply a high-growth technology theme but a strategic capability tied to national competitiveness, industrial upgrading, and long-term productivity. As a result, policy support, capital allocation, and commercialization are unusually aligned, making AI one of the most consequential lenses through which to assess China’s next phase of growth.

China’s Cost-Led AI Strategy

China does not need to lead at the absolute frontier of model performance to succeed. Instead, its strategy emphasizes cost-efficient intelligence and rapid iteration. Open-source models significantly reduce deployment costs, accelerate developer adoption, and enable fast vertical fine-tuning. China now has the lowest application programming interface (API) costs globally, which is powerful because falling costs accelerate the scaling of enterprise and consumer AI use cases. This cost-led approach helps explain the path China has chosen.

Two forces define China’s AI strategy. First, compute constraints. China’s AI capex is less than 20% of that in the United States, largely due to limited access to cutting-edge AI accelerators. Second, application-driven adoption. Even without leading-edge models, China’s growth in daily token consumption is comparable to that of the United States. Both enterprises and consumers are adopting AI at scale, with use cases emerging across a wide range of industries.

To reconcile strong demand with constrained compute, China is pursuing three complementary approaches.

Architectural Efficiency and Model-Level Innovation

Developers are redesigning model architectures to reduce reliance on graphics processing units (GPUs) and high bandwidth memory (HBM) through techniques such as mixture-of-experts, conditional computation, and memory-efficient designs. In addressing compute constraints, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) provides a clear example of architectural innovation. The firm has advanced approaches such as mixture-of-experts and conditional memory to reduce GPU and HBM intensity across AI infrastructure. This is particularly important given that memory remains a key bottleneck and China has yet to achieve a breakthrough in HBM. By decoupling conditional memory from compute and offloading HBM requirements, these designs reduce the need for costly memory upgrades.

System-Level Engineering

This is led most visibly by Huawei. Rather than competing on single-chip specifications, Huawei’s strategy is to win at the system level. Through solutions such as Supernode, hundreds or even thousands of chips are interconnected to deliver competitive performance at the system scale. These architectures rely heavily on optical interconnects, which is why China’s AI infrastructure is notably more optical-intensive than that of the United States.

Domestic Substitution

Local AI chip vendors are expected to gain share, particularly in inference, supported by strong government-led substitution efforts. Inference workloads are more standardized, and economics matter more than peak training performance, allowing “good-enough” accelerators to become competitive more quickly.

From Constraints to Competitive Advantages

Taken together, this framework explains how China can continue to scale AI adoption and commercialization despite constraints at the frontier, turning efficiency, systems engineering, and demand into durable competitive advantages.

We therefore expect adoption to broaden across internet platforms, telecom operators, and data centers backed by state-owned enterprises—an area where government involvement differentiates China from the United States.

The Government’s Role in Scaling AI

China’s government plays a much more active role in shaping the AI and semiconductor ecosystem through three primary mechanisms. First, capital-market support, including green-channel IPO approvals for companies across AI chips, AI models, robotics, and memory. Second, direct capital support, exemplified by the National Integrated Circuit Industry Investment Funds. Third, policy support combined with increasing oversight, as illustrated by recent regulatory intervention in the Meta Manus acquisition.

Demand-Led Adoption and Platform Dynamics

Shifting from the supply side to the demand side, China benefits from a large base of digital-savvy consumers who trust AI and are willing to adopt it quickly. In chatbots, leading platforms—including ByteDance’s Doubao (BDNCE), DeepSeek, and Alibaba’s Qwen (BABA)—each report monthly active users exceeding 100 million.

One visible consequence is the disruption of traditional search behavior: Baidu’s (BIDU) advertising revenue has declined by an estimated 15% to 20% as users migrate from keyword-based search to conversational interfaces.

We believe the next 12 months will be critical for AI agents, with multiple launches expected and Alibaba emerging as an early leader. However, integration remains the primary bottleneck. Cross-platform cooperation is not guaranteed, as illustrated by the recent Duobao AI phone, where attempts to execute tasks across ecosystems ran into limitations because access to third-party applications was restricted or blocked by app operators.

To understand why China’s internet leaders are still able to move quickly despite these frictions, it is helpful to examine their full-stack capabilities. Companies such as Alibaba, ByteDance, and Tencent (TCEHY) are vertically integrated across infrastructure, models, and applications, which shortens the path from model development to deployment and monetization.

From an investor perspective, the key is distribution combined with deployment. We believe platforms that control user traffic, cloud infrastructure, and developer tools are best positioned to scale AI features rapidly and capture value—whether through cloud services, AI-driven advertising innovation, or higher e-commerce conversion rates.

It is also important not to underestimate ByteDance simply because it is privately held. ByteDance is currently the largest spender on AI-related capex in China and is aggressively gaining cloud share, positioning it as a central player—and potential winner—in the AI ecosystem.

Investment Implications Across the AI Stack

In terms of investment implications, increased availability of H200-class accelerators is effectively accelerating capex, as internet companies shift from renting compute to owning capacity. While power availability is not a binding constraint in China, thermal management is, which supports demand for cooling solutions, power management, and continued domestic substitution. We have already discussed memory and connectivity. Beyond this, physical AI is an important theme, as it represents the point at which AI becomes embedded in the real economy rather than remaining confined to digital applications.

In 2026, we expect China’s AI opportunity set to broaden and deepen across the full stack.

Model layer. Chinese large language models are expected to continue iterating rapidly, with ongoing multi-model and agentic releases from major platforms. IPO activity across the AI ecosystem should remain active, supported by a favorable domestic capital-markets backdrop.

Infrastructure layer. Earnings visibility is generally clearer at this level. Priority areas include localization of AI chips, memory, and SIM equipment; optics such as co-packaged optics and optical circuit switching; cooling and power solutions; testers; and server and switch ODMs. These themes are broadly consistent with infrastructure priorities in the U.S. market as well.

Application layer. This is where China has the greatest scope for structural differentiation beyond the major internet platforms, particularly through the rollout of AI agents and new applications. Attractive areas include vertical AI agents for enterprise use cases, ADAS and robotaxi applications, and consumer-facing AI innovations.

Where else do we see growth in China? In parts two and three of this series, Andrey Glukhov and Pierre Horvilleur discuss biotech and advanced manufacturing, respectively.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Discovery Silver: Porcupine Alone Is Worth The Market Cap
data.description:
Discovery Silver: Big Upside Ahead

It's never easy for me to recommend a stock that has tripled in a short period of time, but that's the case here with Discovery Silver Corp. (DSVSF).

While I wish I had bought the stock a year ago and recommended it to readers, I still think it's a buy at current levels. You're not buying into past performance. You're buying into the current company and what this company could look like three to five years from now. And I feel the future opportunity is truly massive.

Today, Discovery is producing roughly 234,000 ounces of gold per year out of three operating mines inside the Porcupine Complex near Timmins, Ontario.

This is a great base to build off of, but it's not the sole reason to buy the stock here. You're buying the stock for what's likely to come, and that's up to 750,000 ounces of annual gold production from Porcupine, plus 14 million ounces of silver annually from Cordero in Mexico. And you're benefitting from meaningful zinc, lead, and copper credits, which bring all-in sustaining costs down to best-in-sector levels.

Porcupine: A $4.7 Billion Mine In Canada

We'll start with Discovery's core asset. The company bought the Porcupine Complex from Newmont (NEM) last year, and management is pretty clear where this project is heading. The company paid total consideration of $425 million for Porcupine; recent NPV estimates peg the value at $4.7 billion, using $4,000/oz gold.

Do the math against a $5.6 billion market cap. Porcupine alone covers nearly the entire company, and it was acquired at a bargain-bin price.

The company's plan is to triple output from the complex, bringing in output from Hoyle Pond, Borden, Pamour, Dome, and TVZ. That's the upside you're getting here. Q4 2025 results also confirmed the cash-flow profile is really strong today, but the future looks more promising.

Overall, I think Discovery got a steal on Porcupine, ultimately paying just a small fraction of the mine complex's true value.

Cordero: One Of The Best Silver Projects In The World

The second part of the investment case here is Discovery's Cordero silver project in Mexico.

Based on the 2024 feasibility study, Cordero should produce roughly 14 million ounces of silver, 208 million pounds of zinc, and 148 million pounds of lead annually over the first ten years of production.

According to this report, zinc and lead byproduct credits will substantially lower its all-in costs. The study pegged AISC at negative $12.50 per silver-equivalent ounce over those first 10 years. Read that number again. Even if inflation pushes capex meaningfully above the feasibility assumptions, Cordero still lands in the first quartile of the global cost curve.

Cordero's NPV came in at $4.6 billion, and that's using a $60/oz silver price, which is obviously below current spot prices; the previous base case in the 2024 study used only $22/oz silver, but $60/oz is definitely a good long-term price to use.

Investors should expect some capex adjustments when accounting for inflation, but the value is certainly strong, and I wouldn't even blink at a 5-10% cost increase.

So, what's the catch? Well, it's obviously not a producing mine, and there's some work to be done.

Cordero is still waiting on its environmental impact assessment ("MIA") from Semarnat, and Mexican mining permits have been a genuine industry bottleneck for years now.

It's also not a cheap project, as Discovery has allocated up to $100 million in expenses this year, most of which will go toward change-of-land-use payments (which will come after MIA approval).

Then it's on to financing, engineering, development, and a 2-year construction period before we start seeing cash flow.

This is a 3+ year out project, meaning that, at best, we will see it producing metals in 2029-30. Given the asset quality and my constructive view on silver, I'm willing to wait.

Kidd: The Quiet Deal That Unlocks Porcupine's Growth

The Kidd deal is one of those acquisitions that looks like a rounding error on the wire but changes the whole story on the ground. Discovery picked up 100% of Glencore's Kidd operations for $10 million up front, assumption of the site obligations, a 1% net smelter return royalty, and a deferred payment of up to $75 million. On paper, that's nothing. Look at what's actually inside the fence, and the deal starts to look like a steal.

What's inside is a four-circuit processing facility, and management has said directly that the mill is the linchpin of its plan to more than double gold output across Timmins. You're also getting a 90-megawatt power supply and access to freshwater. Try permitting that package from scratch in Ontario today. See how it goes. There's residual exposure to copper, zinc, and silver from Kidd Creek on top, plus exploration upside on the district-scale land position. The cost and permitting synergies with Porcupine are real, and the market has not priced them in.

Valuation: Meat Left On The Bone

Discovery screens are expensive at first glance. Around 40 times this year's earnings. That's a rich number for a producer by any traditional yardstick. But earnings multiples are the wrong tool for what this company actually is: a pre-production silver developer bolted onto a mid-tier gold producer with a tripling production plan. The P/E is noise.

Run the sum-of-the-parts instead. Porcupine at $4,000 gold: $4.7 billion. Cordero at $60 silver: $4.6 billion. Add them up, and you're at nearly $10 billion of NPV between the two flagship assets. Market cap today is about $5.6 billion. There's meat on the bone still left, and none of it credits Hoyle Pond, Borden, Pamour, Dome, TVZ, Kidd Creek residuals, or any of the district-scale exploration inventory.

Could metal price assumptions be too generous? I don't think so. Silver is at roughly $80, and gold is trading meaningfully above $4,000 as I write this. If anything, I'm being conservative on both.

Risks: This Is An Execution Story

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. The valuation case above assumes Discovery delivers. If the company executes, gold production doubles, and 14 million ounces of annual silver production eventually shows up on the consolidated statement. If it doesn't execute, you're left with a single operating complex doing 234,000 ounces a year. That does not justify a $5.6 billion market cap.

However, execution risk at Porcupine looks much lower than at Cordero, given that the infrastructure is already in place, and the district is mining-friendly.

At Cordero, Mexican permitting isn't a walk in the park by any means, and seems to have gotten harder in recent years. Mexico was once considered "tier-1" but has since fallen into the tier 2 camp. Recent comments in the Fraser Institute's Annual Survey of Mining Companies (2025 report) seem to confirm this: "Mexico still performs poorly on policy, ranking 53rd of 68 in 2025. All the respondents for Mexico said that security and political stability are major deterrents to investment. Investors also expressed increasing concerns over the availability of skilled labor (+25 points) and trade barriers (+25 points)."

Bottom Line

Discovery Silver isn't a screaming buy and carries some obvious risks, but the upside is real. The company owns an already producing gold mine that's generating robust free cash flow, with the potential to double production using existing infrastructure. Then it also owns one of the highest-quality silver projects on the planet.

If management delivers on its growth strategy, I think the stock doubles from here and this company commands a $10 billion+ market cap. If it stumbles, Discovery likely drifts back toward a $5 handle and gets stuck in a trading range (barring a big move higher in metals).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ServiceNow Q1: The Results Were Fine. That's The Problem
data.description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) stock isn't providing a good service to investors. The earnings report was not enough to stop the free fall in its stock, even with CEO Bill McDermott coming in hot during the earnings call to set the record straight and talk about how well NOW is doing.

The first line from the earnings release reads, “ServiceNow beats high end of guidance across all Q1 2026 top-line growth and profitability metrics, raises full-year subscription revenues outlook.”

Sounds good, but the market said, “Nope. Not good enough.”

Lots of contrarians will want to buy the dip here because of the company's historically low valuation and the overreaction from investors, but this is where I advise caution. Fighting the market isn't pretty. It didn't work when NOW stock went from the $200s all the way down to the low $80s, and there's no major catalyst to tell me that it will work now.

The results were more or less fine. Nothing catastrophic to warrant a 13% drop to $90, but this is investor psychology we're talking about here. Any software stock that doesn't crush estimates will likely be hurt in the next few weeks. “Fine” will not be good enough.

AI is only getting better, and it's hard to keep up with. Investors will keep hearing more AI headlines and sell off most software stocks until further notice. Wait for that trend to change with ServiceNow, unless you're willing to stomach the volatility.

I'm staying on the sidelines with a Hold rating.

Now, let's get into the results.

ServiceNow's Q1 Results In Detail

ServiceNow's non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.97, in line with expectations and up 19.6% year-over-year. Meanwhile, revenue grew 21.3% to $3.77 billion, just barely beating the $3.75 billion consensus. The company tends to beat EPS expectations noticeably, so this quarter's in-line result stands out.

Full-year guidance was also increased for subscription revenues, although margin guidance fell. As you can see below, subscription revenues are forecast at $15.735-15.775 million. Previously, that estimate was $15.53-15.57 million, so the expected growth rate went from 20.5-21% to 22-22.5%.

Not bad on the surface, but management said the guidance includes a 125 bps contribution from Armis, a cybersecurity company it acquired late last year for $7.75B, so that essentially cancels the 150 bps increase in guidance.

The Armis acquisition will also slightly affect margins in the short term. It'll have a 25bps headwind for gross margin, as well as a 75bps headwind for operating margin and a 200bps impact on the free cash flow margin.

That helps explain why non-GAAP subscription gross margin guidance fell from 82% to 81.5%, non-GAAP operating margin fell by 50 bps to 31.5%, and non-GAAP FCF margin fell to 35% from the prior estimate of 36%.

Another thing I'm sure investors noticed was the cRPO number, which fell quarter-over-quarter, although that should be temporary since the 19% cRPO growth forecast for Q2 suggests that it will reach ~$13B next quarter. Nonetheless, 19% expected cRPO growth (or 19.5% on a constant-currency basis) is a slowdown from the usual 20%+.

Investors want to be reassured that the accelerating growth of AI is really a net benefit for the company. That's the problem.

One of the more impressive parts of its earnings report was the one regarding its AI tool, Now Assist: “Now Assist customers spending over $1 million in annual contract value grew over 130% year-over-year.”

That statistic definitely goes right against the “AI will kill ServiceNow” bear case. But again, the market is telling you it doesn't care.

The Stock-Based Compensation Issue

Some people ignore SBC; some don't. But let's be real here. SBC is noticeably affecting GAAP results. While non-GAAP diluted EPS went from $0.81 to $0.97, GAAP EPS went from $0.44 to $0.45. Just one penny higher.

That's partially because stock-based compensation went from $470 million to $547 million year-over-year, a 16.4% increase.

Free cash flow also came in at $1.665 billion, according to the company, but if you calculate FCF the traditional way (cash from operations minus CapEx), you get $1.529 billion. If you remove SBC from that, it becomes $989 million.

ServiceNow's press release mentioned that it bought around 20.1 million shares in Q1, specifically “with the primary objective of managing the impact of dilution.” If you need to buy over 20 million shares just to manage dilution, then I think it's fair to say that SBC is a real expense.

At least management plans to reduce stock-based compensation. Bill McDermott laid out the company's goals during the earnings call, saying:

With a surface area so broad, our goals for ServiceNow are clear. Here they are. Fast time to value for our customers, revenue growth acceleration, margin expansion, reduced stock-based compensation, and outperforming our own rule of 55+ standard.

Those goals are all nice, but we need to see them actually being achieved.

Renewal Rate Fell. Watch It Going Forward

This might not be a big deal, or it could be the start of a longer-term trend. The renewal rate fell from 98% in Q4 to 97%. It was also 97% in Q3 2025, but the footnote says that was due to the closure of a large US federal agency (otherwise, it would have been 98%).

So, this is a real downtick. If you look further back, the renewal rate was 98% in every quarter during 2024 as well. Going further back to 2023, you'll actually see renewal rates between 98% and 99%. 97% is still high, obviously, but investors are probably asking themselves, will this renewal rate continue to drop as AI advances? That's what you need to keep track of. With AI fears being so strong, anything can trigger investors to sell.

The Valuation Has Come Down A Lot

With the stock down to $90 in after-hours trading and TTM non-GAAP EPS of $3.67, NOW stock has a 24.5x P/E ratio.

That's way cheaper than its five-year average of 71.4x. However, it should have never traded at 71x in the first place, and that wasn't a sustainable multiple.

Its price/FCF multiple (using a market cap of ~$93.25 billion and TTM FCF of $4.633 billion) comes in at 20.1x. However, if you want to be conservative and subtract the TTM SBC of $2.032 billion, the multiple climbs to 35.85x.

That's the dilemma. If you think the market will shrug off SBC and focus on the non-GAAP multiple (as it often does, honestly), then the stock is relatively cheap. If not, it's still a bit expensive.

Here are the non-GAAP EPS estimates for the next three years. If the company can actually hit these targets, then the forward multiples for 2026, 2027, and 2028 are 21.5x, 17.9x, and 14.7x, respectively.

I never thought I'd see ServiceNow at these multiples, but there are a few things stopping me from being interested in the stock.

Why I'm Not Buying Despite The Relatively Low Valuation

Here's what's keeping me out. First, NOW is in a downtrend and can't catch a break, even on decent results. That's why it currently has a D- momentum grade from Seeking Alpha's quant score. Not a great setup.

There's also the renewal rate falling, which I'm not ignoring. But mainly, I just don't see a catalyst to reverse the stock's trend, and I would rather not be buying too early.

Some might argue that the Now Assist momentum can help turn the stock around eventually because it's related to AI. However, it hasn't helped yet. The market is ruthless. This reminds me of how Adobe Inc. (ADBE) has been talking about its AI-influenced ARR growing for several quarters now, but the stock is still down.

The Bottom Line On ServiceNow Stock

ServiceNow's results were pretty decent overall, but just not good enough. The market wants to see AI accelerating revenue growth, and we haven't seen it yet. What I would like to see going forward is some revenue acceleration, lower SBC as a percentage of FCF, a stable renewal rate (preferably an uptick to 98%), and the market actually showing that it cares (higher highs and higher lows on the chart).

With no catalysts at the moment and a falling renewal rate, I'd rather stay out of the stock, especially with its strong downtrend.

The relatively low valuation can help limit downside, but that doesn't mean the stock won't chop around for the time being while you pay in opportunity cost.

Therefore, NOW stock gets a Hold from me.

data.title: Eurozone PMI Shows Stagflationary Pressures Increased In April
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte, Chief Economist, Belgium, Luxembourg, Eurozone

Services sector activity tanks while manufacturing companies try to build inventories of crucial inputs

The flash estimate of the composite PMI fell to 48.6 in April, below the boom-or-bust level for the first time in 16 months. The downturn was especially visible in the services sector, where the activity index fell to 47.4, a 62-month low. Somewhat surprisingly, the Manufacturing PMI rose to 52.2, significantly above consensus expectations. While overall new orders decreased for the second month in a row, manufacturing new orders rose at the fastest pace in four years. However, given the supply disruptions caused by the war in the Middle East, it seems that manufacturing activity is currently supported by inventory building, with companies trying to secure crucial inputs in the wake of potential shortages. Although overall growth is clearly slowing, employment decreased only marginally, with staffing levels in the services sector remaining resilient.

Selling prices increase

In the wake of the energy shock and the supply-side disruptions, input costs increased at the fastest level since 2022. Despite the slowdown in growth, output price inflation reached the highest level in 37 months. This is important, as ECB President Christine Lagarde stated during the March press conference that the ECB would be particularly attentive to selling price expectations of firms.

The fact that the S&P website temporarily couldn’t cope with the amount of traffic after the PMI release only illustrates how crucial this report is ahead of next week’s ECB meeting. Lagarde said in a recent speech that the uncertainty about the duration of the shock and the breadth of pass-through argues for gathering more information before drawing firm conclusions for monetary policy. This seems to make a change in interest rates next week still rather unlikely. However, the current PMI report shows that, despite the slowdown, some second-round price effects are already arising. That might push the central bank into at least one rate hike later this year to prevent the current inflation increase from raising inflation expectations.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: CubeSmart Is A Strong Buy
data.description:
I recently shared an article that explained how self-storage REITs could become one of the biggest hidden winners of the AI revolution.

That’s because it is expected to lead to significant labor market disruption, which will then lead to greater mobility as people look for new jobs, creating lots of new demand for self-storage.

Moreover, AI is also making it much easier than ever before to start all sorts of new businesses, and therefore, I expect it to lead to a boom in new small business formation. These new entrepreneurs will not need large industrial warehouses. Rather, they will need a small and flexible self-storage unit for their storage needs.

Finally, because self-storage is one of the most operationally intensive property sectors, it should also benefit a lot from new efficiencies unlocked thanks to AI, potentially leading to higher margins.

All of this has the potential to greatly boost the cash flows of self-storage REITs over the coming five years, and yet, the market does not seem to have yet recognized this long-term opportunity, as most investors remain focused on the near-term challenges caused by oversupply.

It has caused self-storage REITs Public Storage (PSA), Extra Space Storage (EXR), and CubeSmart (CUBE) to crash in recent years, pushing their valuations to historic lows:

I think that this is a great buying opportunity, as these REITs could present significant upside potential as growth accelerates and the narratives shift to how this sector is a hidden winner of AI.

But which self-storage REIT is the best opportunity today?

  • Public Storage?
  • Extra Space Storage?
  • CubeSmart?
  • Or even National Storage Affiliates (NSA)?

I think that it is CUBE. Here are five reasons why:

Reason No. 1: Best Internal Growth Prospects

Its assets enjoy the greatest barriers to entry. They're typically located in densely populated areas, such as Brooklyn, which will limit the long-term growth of new supply and give its assets greater pricing power as the new demand from AI kicks in:

Here's a comparison of all four self-storage REITs:

PSA and EXR also own good assets, but they're far more exposed to future supply risk.

NSA is far behind in terms of asset quality, focusing more on secondary and tertiary markets.

Reason No. 2: Best Long-Term External Growth Prospects

CUBE also stands out in its external growth prospects in two ways.

First, it has a rapidly growing property management business, which does not rely on its own capital. It simply offers management services to other self-storage landlords in exchange for a cut of the profits. It allows it to keep growing even when its own capital is too expensive. It also provides it with a large pipeline of future potential acquisition opportunities.

Second, its size is in the sweet spot of being large enough to enjoy significant economies of scale but not too big, which could slow down its growth.

I fear that PSA and EXR are getting too big for their own good with market caps in excess of $30 billion, making it harder for them to identify enough new investment opportunities that really move the needle.

Market Cap
Public Storage (PSA) $54 billion
Extra Space Storage (EXR) $32 billion
CubeSmart (CUBE) $9 billion

NSA is even smaller than CUBE and could enjoy strong periods of external growth prospects, but its inferior average asset quality will likely lead to greater volatility in its performance and market sentiment, making its access to capital markets less predictable. Moreover, it's now getting bought out by Public Storage anyway, likely ending its chapter as an independent company.

Reason No. 3: Lowest Valuation

Despite owning better assets on average and enjoying better long-term growth prospects, CUBE is quite a bit cheaper than its larger peers, EXR and PSA:

Following its recent surge, NSA is also now trading at a higher P/FFO multiple, despite owning much weaker assets and having quite a bit more leverage:

Reason No. 4: Highest Safe Dividend Yield

As a result of its lower valuation, CUBE is also offering a materially higher dividend yield than EXR and PSA:

NSA yields as much, despite its much higher payout ratio, greater leverage, and lower-quality (higher cap rate) assets.

I have previously predicted that NSA is likely to cut its dividend in 2026 as a result of its high leverage and poor dividend coverage. This will now happen as a result of the all-stock deal, merging with Public Storage, and reducing the dividend of former NSA investors.

CUBE, on the other hand, is safely covering its dividend and just recently even hiked it - a clear signal that its dividend is safe.

Bottom Line

Active REIT investors seeking to maximize total returns may want to favor CUBE.

That does not mean that PSA, EXR, or even NSA will do poorly over time.

It simply means that CUBE is likely to outperform its peer group going forward, and this is why we invest in it at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Toast: More Than Just A POS
data.description:
Many investors see Toast (TOST) as ‘just’ a restaurant Point of Sale provider. However, in recent years the company has broadened its horizons considerably, and can now be viewed as something of a combined POS, fintech, and hardware provider. Moreover, the company is scaling rapidly in terms of new locations, gross payment volume, and ARR. At a reasonable valuation compared to its main listed peer Block (XYZ), and plenty of cash on the balance sheet to fuel growth, the company looks compelling.

Growth Is Broadening

The company’s Q4 and FY 2025 report was jam-packed with solid growth figures, not only in existing markets, but new ones as well. Overall revenue was up 24% for the year driven by a 23% increase in GPV (gross payment volume) and a 26% increase in ARR (annualized recurring run-rate). These are key metrics for what this company is increasingly becoming, namely a combined platform that integrates POS, inventory management and payments. An example of this broader strategy is the recent strategic partnership with Instacart, which will create a “unified local shelf” for businesses to synchronize their physical inventory with the Instacart marketplace.

Free cash flow was especially impressive coming in at $608 million for the year, which means that they converted nearly 10% of their $6.153 billion in revenue and nearly twice their GAAP net income of $342 million into cash. Moreover, GAAP net income was up from $19 million in the previous year for an increase of around 18 times.

Another impressive number was the record 30,000 new net locations, which includes international growth. With operations in Canada, the UK, and Ireland, the company entered Australia as its fourth international market and expects to keep expanding into other countries selectively where they have a “right to compete and win.”

For Q1, analysts are expecting non-GAAP EPS of $0.27, up from $0.20 in the year earlier period for a healthy 35% year-over-year increase but flat sequentially. Management has guided to adjusted EBITDA of $160 to $170 million, while non-GAAP subscription services and financial technology solutions gross profit is expected to rise by 22-24% compared to Q1 2025.

The Bread And Butter

One of Toast’s key strengths is its increasing vertical integration across different revenue streams which now encompasses POS, payments, inventory, payroll, loyalty, and ordering. As such, it is trying to deeply embed itself in the daily operations of restaurants and hospitality businesses, which would increase cross-sell and upsell potential as well as raise switching costs. This is essentially its moat; the fact that customers like the platform, the UI, and the integrated workflows. Management is also investing in AI tools through ToastIQ, which could improve customer retention and raise software monetization over time.

These benefits are also reflected in new partnerships, for example with Papa Murphy’s, which will add 1,000 locations in the US. According to management, owner MTY Food Group likes Toast “for its modern, flexible platform that can support multiple operating models on one system, along with the flexible product features needed for a growing pizza chain”.

Typically, a company at this stage of the growth curve would be reinvesting heavily to accelerate momentum, but Toast is already translating scale into cash, which we can see in free cash flow generation. It currently has more than $2 billion in cash and marketable securities that it can use to fuel further growth, buy back shares as announced, and even fund M&A in the form of tuck-ins or a full strategic acquisition. Management has not said much about M&A, so this remains speculative at this point.

Valuation Looks Reasonable

The company currently trades at a FWD Non-GAAP P/E of 22.67, which is higher than Block’s 20.5, but not overly so. It’s also not much more expensive on revenue-based metrics, with Toast at 2.76x versus Block’s 1.87x. The premium can be explained through the higher growth Toast enjoys, with a 5-year revenue CAGR of 49.5% versus Block’s 20.5%. Where Toast doesn’t look as attractive is FWD EV/EBITDA at 18.8x, a clear premium to Block’s 9.8 and nearly double the sector median of 9.7.

As management has indicated, profitability is not yet mature as the company continues to invest in top line expansion. As such, the company’s gross margins are not in line with Block yet. Block has a superior gross margin of 43% versus Toast’s 26%, which is partly due to the companies’ different revenue mix. However, they’re almost equal on net income margin around 5.5%. This is encouraging given the fact that Toast has not switched to full monetization mode yet.

Risks & Catalysts

The company remains heavily exposed to the restaurant and hospitality sector, which can be sensitive to slower consumer spending especially with inflation being sticky, as well as wage inflation and failure rates among smaller retail operators. Another risk is continued competition in the space, as Block, Clover, and legacy providers all compete for market share which may result in pricing pressure.

On the catalyst side, continued 20%+ ARR growth, strong net location additions, and further free cash flow expansion could support upside. Additional restaurant chain adds, successful international launches, and accelerated share repurchases backed by its $2 billion liquidity position could also improve sentiment.

Conclusion

Toast occupies an interesting niche in the vertical SaaS sector, as it combines POS, payments, inventory management, payroll and other services in one package. Growth remains strong on the basis of overall revenue, GPV, new locations, and ARR, and the company is still scaling. Meanwhile, roughly $2 billion in cash and marketable securities gives Toast plenty of opportunities for expansion, buybacks, and reinvestment. Valuation doesn’t look cheap on every metric, but it looks reasonable for the kind of growth that the company is delivering. With an attractive mix of growth, earnings quality, and balance sheet strength, I rate Toast a Buy.

data.title: Dollar Reasserts Itself As Global Tensions Shift Currency Markets
data.description:
By Jim Iuorio

For over a year, the U.S. dollar has been under sustained pressure. Then, in the first week of March, it suddenly found its footing, gaining over 2.5% against both the euro and the yen, and then, by March 16, 3% against the euro. The question isn't whether the dollar rallied, but rather how sustainable the move is and what it means for the broader currency landscape heading into the back half of 2026.

The catalyst was geopolitical. On Saturday, February 28, the United States launched a military offensive on Iran. Markets responded with sharp volatility across asset classes, but the most telling moves came in foreign exchange. The dollar, which had been steadily losing ground, abruptly reversed course. What followed was a chain reaction through energy markets, inflation expectations, Fed policy pricing and ultimately, currency positioning across the globe.

The Setup: A Year of Dollar Weakness

To understand the reversal, it helps to step back and look at the last two years. The euro had surged from $1.02 in early 2025 to $1.21 by January 2026 — an 18% gain driven by expectations of aggressive Fed easing, concerns about U.S. trade policy pushing capital away from American assets and the unwinding of a massive long-dollar positioning from late 2024. The dollar was under sustained selling pressure, and markets were positioning for continued weakness.

Then oil spiked, and everything changed.

Oil, Inflation and the Fed Reassessment

Conflict in the Middle East triggered a sharp increase in oil prices. WTI crude oil futures rose from roughly $65 per barrel to over $119 by the March 9 trading session — an 84% gain in just over a week. That kind of upward volatility has implications far beyond the pump.

Two-year inflation breakevens spiked from 2.8% to 3.2% as markets reassessed the inflation outlook. More critically, expectations for Fed easing plummeted. Prior to the conflict, the CME FedWatch tool showed the market pricing roughly 50 basis points of rate cuts by year-end 2026. That number decreased to approximately 0 basis points of cuts by March 23.

The thesis behind this shift seems fairly straightforward: the oil spike was inflationary, and the Fed might not have the runway to ease into a softening labor market without risking a resurgence in price pressures. Higher U.S. rates, or at least the expectation of less accommodation, are a structural tailwind for the dollar. While German yields also moved higher, the differential between U.S. policy rates and those of other major economies widened materially. That's a key fundamental driver behind the dollar's recent strength.

So was this a safe-haven bid? The move doesn’t appear to be purely about safe-haven flows. The dollar's 2.5% gain against both the euro and the yen was significant but not necessarily strong enough to suggest pure panic buying. If safe-haven demand were the dominant force, one might expect a more explosive reaction, particularly from the yen.

Yet, there were signs of heightened risk aversion — Euro currency CVOL, a CME Group index that measures implied volatility in the euro, spiked over 30% after the conflict. While this was a substantial move, it came off of an 18-month low for the index, suggesting measured concern rather than an indication of panic.

The better explanation is a combination: some flight-to-quality demand, layered on top of a rate differential story that was already starting to favor the U.S.

This interpretation is supported by the CME Commitment of Traders (COT) tool, which showed corresponding institutional liquidation of long Euro positions that had been established during the previous period of Euro strength.

What About Other Currencies?

The broader dollar story is playing out unevenly across different currency pairs.

The yen's position is particularly precarious. Japan imports virtually all of its energy, so sustained high crude prices hit its terms of trade directly. The Bank of Japan has kept rates near zero while other central banks have tightened, leaving the yen vulnerable to widening rate differential.

Commodity-linked currencies like the Australian and Canadian dollar have seen some support from higher oil prices, but that's been offset by risk-off sentiment and concerns about global growth. The Aussie is particularly sensitive to Chinese demand, and with China's economy still navigating structural headwinds, AUD hasn't been able to capitalize on the commodity rally.

Emerging market (EM) currencies are another story. Higher U.S. rates and a stronger dollar create pressure for EM central banks, many of which are dealing with their own inflation challenges. If the dollar continues to strengthen, we could see renewed stress in countries with dollar-denominated debt or trade imbalances.

The British pound has been relatively resilient, trading in a narrow range against the dollar. The Bank of England has maintained a cautious stance, and while UK inflation has moderated, it hasn't collapsed to the degree that would force aggressive easing. The pound’s near-term path will likely track the euro-dollar — if the euro weakens materially, the pound probably follows, though to a lesser degree.

The Path Forward

The dollar's trajectory over the rest of 2026 will likely be shaped by three forces: monetary policy divergence, geopolitical risk and U.S. trade policy. Right now, the first two favor the dollar, while the third cuts against it. Below are three potential scenarios to consider:

  • Scenario 1: Sustained Tension and Dollar Strength - If tensions remain elevated and oil stays above $100, the Fed's limited easing path and safe-haven flows support continued dollar strength.
  • Scenario 2: Stabilization and Range-Bound Trading - If geopolitical risk ebbs and oil stabilizes, the dollar likely consolidates recent gains in a range-bound pattern. Market participants would reassess Fed policy expectations based on incoming economic data.
  • Scenario 3: Resolution and Renewed Weakness - A quick resolution to Middle East tensions coupled with deteriorating U.S. economic data could push the Fed back toward aggressive easing and renewed dollar weakness.

Positioning matters too. The massive build-up of euro longs during 2025's rally hasn't entirely unwound. If safe-haven buying continues and those positions get squeezed, euro liquidation could accelerate the dollar’s move lower. From a technical perspective, the euro-dollar is testing key support around $1.15, with a break below opening the door to $1.10. For dollar-yen, the ¥150 level is psychologically important.

Trading The Move

For those looking to hedge currency exposure or express a directional view, CME Group offers efficient tools across multiple pairs. Euro FX futures provide meaningful capital efficiency at nearly 50-to-1 leverage, while Micro contracts (one-tenth the size) allow for tighter risk management. Similar Micro contracts exist for the yen, pound and other major currencies.

The most prudent posture for traders is cautious and nimble. The landscape can shift quickly, and proper position sizing matters more than usual in this environment. The dollar is having a moment — whether it's a short-term bounce or the beginning of a sustained reversal will depend on how the next few months unfold.

Original Post

data.title: UFP Technologies: Great Growth At Just An Okay Price
data.description:
When looking for new and interesting companies to analyze, one firm that came across my radar was UFP Technologies (UFPT). This is an intriguing business, serving as a developer and producer of single use and single patient medical devices. This is a massive industry as you will see shortly. And it is one that the business has done quite well in over the last few years. Revenue, profits, and cash clothes, have all risen nicely. And relative to other similar firms, the stock is trading at levels that are quite attractive. The only reason, honestly, why I cannot turn bullish on the business right now is because, on an absolute basis, the stock looks more fairly valued than it does undervalued. But it wouldn't take much for me to change my opinion here. If we see the share price drop another 10%, or if we see rapid growth throughout this year, that could easily bridge the gap. But for now, I think that calling it a 'hold' candidate makes the most sense here.

A great business at just an okay price

As I mentioned already, UFP Technologies operates as a producer of single use and single patient medical devices. I know this is kind of amorphous. So it would be helpful to dig more into precisely what it does. For instance, the company produces custom medical products such as protective drapes for robotic surgeries, patient handling and comfort, advanced wound care, infection prevention, disposables that are used in surgical and endoscopic procedures, and even packaging for medical devices and orthopedic implants. There are other examples as well such as cardiac implant components, dispenser coils used in the production of catheters, and more. But you get the idea.

Management has made clear that this is a massive space to play in. The MedTech industry as the company calls it is worth about $550 billion globally. This industry includes the global medical device CDMO market that is worth about $101 billion and that's growing at an annualized rate of about 10%. But as a whole, the MedTech industry is growing at a rate of about 6% annually. Perhaps the most exciting category in this market would be the surfaces and support industry that is responsible for about 23% of the company's medical sales. It's growing at a low double digit rate. But there are other components of the space that are growing attractively also. This includes the infection prevention market, the orthopedics market, the therapeutics market, the wound care market, and the diagnostics market, all of which are growing at a mid single digit rate. The robotic surgery industry that is responsible for about 29% of medical sales is growing at a mid teens rate. So clearly, the company has a lot going for it.

When it comes specifically to the robotic surgery industry, this is a market that, over the next several years, should do exceptionally well. In 2024, it was worth approximately $11 billion. But it should more than doubled to $23.7 billion by 2029. This is in spite of the fact that we already have a large number of surgeries being conducted by this means already. There are more than 300 million major surgeries performed annually using robotics. And even when you look at the soft surgery market, that number is a solid 44 million. As the leading provider of several materials and capabilities that are necessary for this space, UFP Technologies stands to benefit from continued expansion. And of course, the firm is targeting other opportunities as well. This includes not only expanding its business with existing customers, such as through the addition of new value added services, but through additional innovation that increases its own capabilities.

Management is also not afraid to invest in acquisition activities. In July of last year, for instance, the company acquired Techno Plastics Industries, which operates as a specialty producer of precision thermoplastic injection molded components. That was a rather small purchase, costing the company about $4.3 million. But also at around the same time, the company purchased Universal Plastics & Engineering Company, a producer of precision thermoformed and heat sealed polymer components that are used for shielding batteries and certain implantable medical devices. That ended up costing about $7.6 million. There are other acquisitions as well, but you get the idea.

Management has taken advantage of the opportunities presented by this growing market to expand nicely over the last few years. From 2023 through 2025, revenue jumped from $400.1 million to $602.8 million. Most of that growth seems to have been the result of acquisitions that the company made over the last couple of years. In fact, for 2025, companies acquired in 2024 and 2025 brought in $168.3 million of revenue. This is not to say that the firm has not been capable of achieving organic growth. Organic revenue actually increased by 1.5% in 2025.

As revenue for the company has expanded, profitability has increased also. Over the last three years, net profits surged from $44.9 million to $68.3 million. But that's not all. Adjusted net income grew from $51 million to $76.1 million. Operating cash flow more than doubled from $41.3 million to $91.9 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $65.5 million to $101.6 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $77.2 million to $121.1 million.

From what I can tell, management has not provided comprehensive guidance for 2026. However, they have expressed that further growth is on the horizon. This should come in part from new program launches, including those in the patient services market that involve infection prevention and robotic surgery activities. Those are expected to be the primary drivers of organic expansion for the year. The firm is not focused only on growth. It is also working on reducing overall expenses. One example would be operating inefficiencies at its AJR facility. Management mentioned in their annual report what they refer to as the 'AJR Labor Issue'. After the company acquired AJR Enterprises in 2024, it conducted a review of the legal work eligibility of employees there. The end result here was employee turnover of more than 50% of the workforce there. This then required the company to hire and train replacement workers that ended up negatively impacting productivity. But the good news is that management expects those troubles to be drawing to a close. For instance, they highlighted in their latest earnings call that they have made progress in that regard and they expect the impact of inefficiencies to diminish even more in the first quarter of this year and beyond. So that is excellent to see.

Given how the company has performed in recent years, it's awfully tempting to be bullish about it. I certainly am from an operational standpoint. But, from a valuation standpoint, I cannot pull the trigger on a bullish assessment. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using results from 2024 and 2025. These certainly aren't lofty valuations. But they do fit into the fair value category as I typically describe it. Relative to other similar firms, it is worth noting that the stock is a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In that same chart, you can see that only one company ended up being cheaper than it is on a price to earnings basis. One was cheaper and another was tied with it on a price to operating cash flow basis. And then, on an EV to EBITDA basis, two firms ended up being cheaper.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I must say that I am optimistic from an operational standpoint when it comes to UFP Technologies. But I cannot be bullish on it from a valuation standpoint. Yes, the company is attractively priced compared to other similar firms. But relative valuation only goes so far with me. I like opportunities that are basically a guaranteed bet or that are so discounted that a lot would have to go wrong in order for me to underperform the market. And even though I expect the business to continue expanding for the foreseeable future, I just don't see that kind of situation here. Because of that, I think that rating it a 'hold' is the right choice right now.

data.title: Adobe: One Of The Cheapest Franchises In Software
data.description:
Introduction

Adobe (ADBE) has been one of the most punished software stock starting the year with the rise of AI tools as it lost nearly 27% YTD and around 60% of its all-time high, reducing its overall market cap of $310B to now $100B. This is based on the narrative that models are commoditizing image generation and making Photoshop irrelevant, while competitors such as Canva are eating away at Creative Cloud. I’ll dive into why I believe Adobe still represents an opportunity in today’s market.

Current Dynamics

I first want to dive into the latest earnings as they capture the current disconnect between the perceived value of Adobe with what has been really happening on the market. Revenue came out to $6.4B during fiscal Q1, which is actually up 12% Y/Y, beating the street estimates by around $120MM. Subscription revenue itself grew by 12%, while business professionals & consumers are at $1.78B, a 16% increase. Non-GAAP EPS reached $6.06, beating the consensus of $5.86 meanwhile, operating cash flow set a record at $2.96B, up 19% Y/Y. Looking ahead, management has guided for a full-year revenue of between $25.9 and $26.1B and up to $23.5 in non-GAAP EPS meaning that the company is definitely beating all doom expectations.

What I want to say is that, simply, these are not the numbers of a company facing an imminent decline. The business has continued to grow its revenue in the AI age at around 10.75% CAGR over the past 3 years, while at the same time expanding its FCF margins. Basically, the valuation got ahead of itself and the firm has been paying for it, though it now boasts the smallest P/E in its recent history at 14.85.

Adobe has also gone through a pivot that has been underappreciated, as the firm is now targeting agentic orchestration, meaning that Adobe has repositioned its entire Creative Cloud suite as an operating system for agents instead of focusing on singular features such as Generative Fill or text-to-image. For example, the Firefly AI assistant maintains persistent memory across sessions and can execute over 100 pre-built creative skills such as batch-editing lighting across an entire video timeline in Premiere Pro.

This is basically a strong defensive response to the effective commoditization of image generation as it integrates AI at the orchestration layer, preserving Adobe’s pixel-perfect moat. It also allows professional creators to maintain a streak between conversational AI guidance and manual and high-fidelity editing. This all ensures that Adobe remains the professional standard.

The collaboration with Anthropic (ANTHRO) is also a strong, pivotal strategic move within this agentic era, as Adobe will integrate Firefly AI assistant directly into Anthropic’s Claude chatbot, Adobe is meeting creators where they ideate. This open ecosystem approach, which now extends with OpenAI, Google Cloud and Microsoft, is basically laying the foundation for the future of software interaction through AI-interfaces.

Adobe has also decided to acquire Semrush for $1.9B in a push into the Digital Experience sector’s new frontier, being generative engine optimization. Semrush will provide Adobe with the tracking and monitoring capabilities necessary to manage brands' visibility inside these answer engines. Basically, it provides tools to ensure that a company’s website is machine-recommendable which is mission-critical for 99% of the firm’s customers.

Valuation

As I mentioned earlier, Adobe is now trading at some of its lowest multiples in its recent history, with a P/E Non-GAAP of 11.27, drastically lower than any of its peers, such as Microsoft (MSFT) at 27.58 or even Salesforce (CRM) at 14.91. The same trend could be seen with the firm’s EV/EBITDA at 10.48 and what is even more astonishing would be the firm's free cash flow generation that now translates to a yield of around 10%, which is simply astonishing in today’s software market. The price is also the lowest we have seen since 2018, when the firm was only generating $9B in revenue. I also did a scenario analysis of the firm and a DCF to also grasp the undervaluation of shares at the current prices.

On a scenario-based view, even my most bearish thesis would imply a share price of $258, an actual upside from today’s prices. On a probability weighted target, the share price would reach a strong $422, or around a 65.5% upside from here. On a DCF model base, the implied share price is closer to $312, based on a high WACC of 10.6% and a terminal growth of 2%, implying an upside of 26.4%.

I’m the most bullish on the launch of the Firefly AI assistant during the month that is basically one of the first creative agents capable of orchestrating multi-step tasks across the entire Creative Cloud suite from a single conversational interface. Adobe is also expanding into the Experience Cloud with Adobe Brand Concierge, which is an AI-powered conversational application that turns static websites into dynamic and personalized shopping experiences. With apparently shoppers who arrive at retail sites via AI chatbots are 38% more likely to make a purchase as there are more intent to buy, while Bain is estimating that the US agentic commerce market could reach $300B to $500B by 2030, representing up to 25% of all e-commerce.

Furthermore, despite the doom and gloom of AI on Adobe’s business, margins have effectively improved since the launch of AI as gross margin went from 87.7% to 89.4%, while net went from 27% to 29.5%. More so, free cash flow margin, which dipped to 35.8% back in 2023, has now reached 42.4%, which is simply enormous for a company of that size. This all allows Adobe to have great return metrics, such as an astonishing ROE of 62.5%, while ROCE is also great at 48.9%.

Risks

There is a leadership transition risk as the departure of longtime CEO Shantanu Narayen caused the stock to drop. The market saw the loss of his steady hand who navigated the company’s historic cloud pivot as a negative and a botched succession process or a radical shift in strategy under an outsider hire could continue to scare the market.

The legacy high-margin stock photography business is eroding faster than management anticipated due to the rise of gen AI alternatives. In the company’s Q1, this decline was responsible for a $70MM drag on net new ARR and if the erosion of these legacy assets continues to outpace the monetization of new AI tools, it will definitely suppress net new ARR for several more quarters.

Conclusion

I believe that the overall SaaS sector selloff has been overblown and has hit Adobe the hardest, while it is one of the most enterprise-entrenched software companies out there. The company has recorded record cash flows and a super aggressive $25B buyback strategy that signals that Adobe is through the worst of the transition volatility. Though the leadership transition and the stock business drag remain near-term hurdles, the underlying engine that is Creative Cloud is effectively accelerating into the AI era. I rate Adobe as a Strong Buy at these prices.

data.title: French Business Confidence Confirms Weakness In Domestic Demand
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

Deteriorating growth outlook

As expected, business confidence in France deteriorated sharply in April, down three points over the month. The decline is particularly pronounced in retail trade, where the indicator fell by six points, as well as in services. In both sectors, the overall outlook has darkened significantly: expected demand is declining, and order intentions have contracted sharply.

By contrast, business confidence remained stable in construction and improved slightly in industry. In this sector, overall order books are recovering, as are firms’ own production expectations. A similar pattern is observed in the PMI indicators, with the services PMI deteriorating sharply, while the manufacturing PMI improved.

The increased vulnerability of Asian industrial competitors to the current energy crisis (due to their heavy reliance on supplies transiting through the Strait of Hormuz) appears to be supporting the competitiveness of French firms. In addition, the risk of shortages and price rises seems to be prompting industrial clients to bring forward their orders. This is a clearly positive signal for French industrial conditions. However, its durability remains uncertain and is closely tied to developments in the geopolitical situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

This support for industry will help the French economy limit the immediate risk of slipping into recession. However, it will not be sufficient to offset the deterioration in domestic demand, which is very visible in the April data. Rising energy prices appear to be weighing heavily on household purchasing behaviour. They are curbing consumption, and this is directly weakening activity in retail trade and services. This weakness in domestic demand will significantly constrain economic growth this year. We now forecast growth of 0.7% in France in 2026, compared with 1% expected before the outbreak of the conflict. This forecast could be revised further downwards if the conflict were to drag on even more.

Inflation set to rise in the coming months

Business confidence indicators also point to a recovery in firms' price expectations across all sectors. The rebound is particularly visible in industry and construction. Nevertheless, expected prices remain well below their 2022 levels, once again illustrating the difference in impact of this energy crisis compared with the previous one. Domestic demand simply appears too weak to trigger price increases similar to those observed at the time.

Moreover, the risk of an inflationary spiral remains more contained in France than in the rest of the euro area. The starting point for inflation is lower than in most European countries. The French economy is characterised by a large share of administered or centrally negotiated prices, which slows the pass-through of cost increases to consumer prices. In addition, the labour market remains fragile. Hiring intentions are weak, and net job creation has been negative for several quarters. Ongoing wage negotiations do not fully incorporate this new inflationary shock. In this context, wage growth is likely to remain more moderate than elsewhere in the euro area, limiting inflationary pressures, especially in services.

This does not, however, imply that inflation will remain under control. Rising energy prices, higher costs for certain key inputs (such as fertilisers or metals), and the rebound in firms’ pricing intentions suggest that French inflation should continue to rise and exceed 2% for several months this year. At this stage, we expect average HICP inflation of 2.1% in 2026, compared with 1.3% in our pre-conflict forecast.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
